In Short: Pre-eclampsia is hypertension (≥140/90 mmHg) with proteinuria after 20 weeks. Eclampsia = pre-eclampsia + convulsions. 4 stages: premonitory, tonic, clonic, coma. MgSO4 (Pritchard regimen) is drug of choice. Delivery = definitive cure.

Definitions

Pre-eclampsia: New-onset hypertension (BP ≥140/90) + proteinuria (≥300mg/24h) after 20 weeks.

Eclampsia: Pre-eclampsia + generalised tonic-clonic convulsions not attributable to other causes.

Four Stages of Eclamptic Convulsion

StageDurationFeaturesNursing Action
1. Premonitory15–30 secFacial twitching, rolling eyes, fixed stareCall help; left lateral; remove dentures
2. Tonic15–20 secBody rigid; teeth clenched; opisthotonos; apnoeaDo NOT insert anything in mouth; maintain airway
3. Clonic1–2 minViolent rhythmic jerking; frothy saliva; incontinenceProtect from injury; turn to side; suction
4. ComaMin to hoursDeep unconsciousness; may progress to next seizureMonitor vitals q5min; prepare MgSO4; assess FHR

Medical Management

  • MgSO4 (Pritchard):
    • Loading: 4g IV over 15–20 min + 5g IM each buttock (total 14g)
    • Maintenance: 5g IM q4h alternate buttock for 24h after last convulsion
    • Monitor RUK: Respiration ≥16, Urine ≥30mL/hr, Knee jerk present
    • Antidote: Calcium gluconate 10% — 10 mL IV slowly
  • Antihypertensive: Labetalol 20mg IV or Nifedipine 10mg oral
  • Delivery = definitive cure. Plan within 12–24h of stabilisation
  • Supportive: IV RL 60–80 mL/hr; Foley catheter; O2 4–6 L/min

Nursing Management

  • BP every 15 min; urine output hourly; LOC; DTR; FHR monitoring
  • During convulsion: Left lateral + suction; protect from injury; do NOT restrain
  • Post-convulsion: Quiet environment; continue MgSO4; prepare for delivery
  • Danger signs: Absent knee jerk, RR <16, urine <30 mL/hr, recurrent convulsions
Mnemonic: MgSO4 monitoring — "RUK"
RUK
R = Respiration (≥16/min) | U = Urine (≥30 mL/hr) | K = Knee jerk (present)
Any absent → WITHHOLD MgSO4 + give calcium gluconate
Quick Revision
  • 4 stages: Premonitory → Tonic → Clonic → Coma
  • Drug of choice: MgSO4 (Pritchard: 4g IV + 10g IM)
  • Antidote: Calcium gluconate 10%
  • Delivery = definitive cure
Keywords: EclampsiaMgSO4PritchardRUKCalcium gluconateConvulsion stages

In Short: Multiple pregnancy = two or more fetuses. Twins: dizygotic (70%, 2 eggs) or monozygotic (30%, 1 egg splits). Complications: preterm, PIH, anaemia, PPH, TTTS. Management: intensive ANC, serial USG, planned delivery at 36–37 weeks.

Definition

Multiple Pregnancy: Presence of two or more fetuses in the uterus simultaneously.

Types of Twins

FeatureDizygotic (70%)Monozygotic (30%)
Origin2 ova + 2 sperm1 ovum splits
PlacentaAlways DCDA (2 placentas)DCDA, MCDA, or MCMA
SexSame or differentAlways same
GeneticsDifferent (like siblings)Identical
HeredityRuns in familiesRandom

Maternal Complications

  • Anaemia (doubled iron demand)
  • PIH/pre-eclampsia (3× risk)
  • Polyhydramnios
  • Preterm labour (50% before 37 wks)
  • PPH (overdistended uterus → atony)
  • Higher caesarean rate
  • Hyperemesis (high hCG)

Fetal Complications

  • Prematurity (most common)
  • IUGR/discordant growth
  • TTTS (in monochorionic)
  • Malpresentation
  • Cord entanglement (MCMA)
  • Congenital anomalies (×2 risk)

Management

  • Confirm chorionicity by USG <14 weeks (λ-sign = DCDA; T-sign = MCDA)
  • Double IFA; extra protein and calcium
  • Fortnightly visits from 24 wks; weekly from 32 wks
  • Serial growth USG q2–4 weeks
  • MCDA: fortnightly Doppler from 16 wks for TTTS
  • Delivery: DCDA at 37 wks, MCDA at 36 wks, MCMA at 32–34 wks
  • Hospital delivery with OT, blood bank, NICU
  • AMTSL mandatory (high PPH risk)
Mnemonic: Complications — "PAPH"
P=PIH | A=Anaemia | P=Polyhydramnios/Preterm | H=Hyperemesis/Higher C-section
Quick Revision
  • Multiple pregnancy = two or more fetuses
  • Twins: dizygotic (70%, 2 eggs) or monozygotic (30%, 1 egg splits)
  • Complications: preterm, PIH, anaemia, PPH, TTTS
  • Management: intensive ANC, serial USG, planned delivery at 36–37 weeks.
Keywords: Multiple pregnancyDizygoticMonozygoticDCDAMCDATTTSPretermPPH

In Short: Twins classified by zygosity (mono/dizygotic) and chorionicity (DCDA/MCDA/MCMA). Chorionicity determines management and risk. DCDA safest; MCMA most dangerous. USG before 14 weeks is essential.

Chorionicity Types

TypeWhen SplitPlacentaMembraneRiskDelivery
DCDADay 0–32 separateThick (λ sign)Lowest37 wks
MCDADay 4–81 sharedThin (T-sign)Moderate (TTTS)36 wks
MCMADay 8–131 sharedNONEHighest (cord tangle)32–34 wks
ConjoinedDay >131 sharedNone (fused)Extremely rareSurgical

Management

  • Early USG <14 weeks to determine chorionicity
  • Double-dose IFA; extra calories and protein
  • MCDA: screen for TTTS every 2 weeks from 16 wks
  • Serial growth USG to detect discordant growth (>20% weight difference)
  • Corticosteroids if preterm delivery expected before 34 wks
  • OT and NICU on standby for delivery
Mnemonic: Split timing — "0-3-8-13"
0–3 = DCDA (safest) | 4–8 = MCDA (TTTS risk) | 8–13 = MCMA (cord entangle) | >13 = Conjoined

In Short: Normal cord: 50 cm, 2 arteries, 1 vein, Wharton’s jelly. Abnormalities: short/long cord, single umbilical artery, true knots, nuchal cord, cord prolapse, velamentous insertion, vasa praevia.

Cord Abnormalities

AbnormalitySignificance
Short cord (<30 cm)Abruption, uterine inversion
Long cord (>75 cm)Prolapse, true knots, nuchal cord
Single umbilical artery20% have fetal anomalies (renal, cardiac)
True knotCord occlusion → fetal death if tightened
Nuchal cordCommon (25%); usually harmless; tight loops cause distress
Cord prolapseEmergency — cord compression → fetal death
Velamentous insertionVessels unprotected; rupture risk during ARM
Vasa praeviaVessels cross internal os → fetal haemorrhage at ROM

Nursing Points

  • After delivery: examine cord for 2 arteries + 1 vein
  • Single artery → neonatal investigation for anomalies
  • Variable decelerations on CTG suggest cord compression
  • Cord prolapse: knee-chest position + push presenting part UP
Mnemonic: Cord contents — "AVA"
A=Artery (deoxy) | V=Vein (oxy) | A=Artery. 2 arteries + 1 vein = normal.
Quick Revision

  • Abnormalities: short/long cord, single umbilical artery, true knots, nuchal cord, cord prolapse, velamentous insertion, vasa praevia.

In Short: IUGR = estimated fetal weight <10th percentile. Two types: symmetric (20%, early, all organs small) and asymmetric (80%, late, brain-sparing, head > abdomen). Doppler worsening: high RI → AEDF → REDF → DV abnormal. Management: treat cause, serial monitoring, corticosteroids, timely delivery.

Definition

IUGR: Fetus fails to achieve genetic growth potential — EFW or AC <10th percentile for gestational age.

Etiology

CategoryCauses
MaternalPIH, anaemia, malnutrition, smoking, chronic renal disease, SLE
FetalChromosomal (trisomy 13,18,21), TORCH infections, congenital anomalies
PlacentalPlacental insufficiency, placenta praevia, abruption, circumvallate placenta
EnvironmentalHigh altitude, radiation, teratogenic drugs

Types

FeatureSymmetric (20%)Asymmetric (80%)
OnsetEarly (1st trimester)Late (3rd trimester)
CauseGenetic, TORCHPlacental insufficiency, PIH
ProportionsAll parts equally smallHead spared, abdomen small
HC/AC ratioNormalIncreased (>1)
MechanismFewer cells (hypoplasia)Smaller cells; brain-sparing
PrognosisPoorerBetter (catch-up possible)

Diagnosis

  • SFH <expected for GA
  • USG: EFW <10th percentile; AC <10th percentile
  • Doppler: UA → AEDF → REDF = very ominous
  • MCA: increased diastolic flow (brain-sparing)
  • CPR (MCA PI/UA PI): <1 = abnormal
  • DV: absent/reversed a-wave = cardiac compromise
  • BPP score ≤4 = urgent delivery

Management

  • Treat cause (control PIH, treat anaemia, nutrition)
  • Serial USG q2 weeks; UA Doppler weekly
  • NST/CTG twice weekly from 32 wks
  • Betamethasone 12mg IM ×2 if delivery before 34 wks
  • Normal Doppler: deliver at 37–38 wks
  • AEDF: deliver at 32–34 wks
  • REDF or abnormal DV: urgent delivery
  • Left lateral position 2h/day (improves blood flow)
Mnemonic: Doppler progression — "HARD"
H=High resistance | A=Absent end-diastolic flow | R=Reversed flow | D=Ductus venosus abnormal → URGENT delivery
Quick Revision
  • IUGR = estimated fetal weight <10th percentile
  • Two types: symmetric (20%, early, all organs small) and asymmetric (80%, late, brain-sparing, head > abdomen)
  • Doppler worsening: high RI → AEDF → REDF → DV abnormal
  • Management: treat cause, serial monitoring, corticosteroids, timely delivery.
Keywords: IUGRFGRSymmetricAsymmetricDopplerAEDFREDFBrain-sparingCPR

In Short: IUFD = fetal death after 28 weeks before delivery. Common causes: PIH, abruption, IUGR, cord accidents. Diagnosis: absent FM + no FHR + USG confirmation. Management: misoprostol induction; watch for DIC. Emotional support is the key nursing role.

Causes

  • Maternal: PIH/eclampsia, severe anaemia, diabetes, infections (malaria, syphilis)
  • Fetal: Congenital anomalies, Rh isoimmunisation, chromosomal
  • Placental: Abruptio placentae, placental insufficiency, cord accidents
  • Unexplained: 25–50% have no identifiable cause

Diagnosis

  • Mother reports absent fetal movements >12 hours
  • No FHR on Doppler
  • USG: absent cardiac activity (definitive); Spalding sign (overlapping skull bones)
  • Investigations: CBC, coagulation profile (rule out DIC)

Management

  • Induction: vaginal misoprostol 25–50 mcg q4–6h
  • If retained >4 weeks: risk of DIC
  • Correct coagulopathy with FFP, cryoprecipitate if DIC
  • Send placenta and fetus for post-mortem
  • Suppress lactation: cabergoline 1mg single dose
  • Emotional support: Allow grieving; privacy; counselling; allow mother to see baby
Quick Revision
  • IUFD = fetal death after 28 weeks before delivery
  • Common causes: PIH, abruption, IUGR, cord accidents
  • Diagnosis: absent FM + no FHR + USG confirmation
  • Management: misoprostol induction; watch for DIC
Keywords: IUFDSpalding signDICMisoprostolBereavement care

In Short: Obstructed labour = presenting part cannot descend despite adequate contractions due to mechanical barrier. Signs: Bandl’s ring, moulding +3, maternal exhaustion. Management: stop oxytocin, IV fluids, catheterise, emergency C-section.

Definition

Obstructed Labour: Labour where, despite adequate contractions, the fetus cannot be delivered vaginally due to mechanical barrier from passenger, passage, or both.

Causes

Passage (Maternal)Passenger (Fetal)
Contracted pelvis (most common)Hydrocephalus
Pelvic tumour (fibroid)Macrosomia (>4 kg)
Cervical stenosisMalpresentation (brow, face MP)
Vaginal septumMalposition (deep transverse arrest)
Previous pelvic fractureShoulder dystocia

Clinical Features

  • Bandl’s ring: Visible retraction ring — impending uterine rupture
  • Moulding +3 (irreducible skull bone overlap)
  • Maternal distress: tachycardia, dehydration, fever
  • Fetal distress or absent FHR
  • Oedematous vulva and cervix
  • Blood-stained urine (bladder compression)

Complications

MaternalFetal
Uterine rupture (most dangerous)Fetal death (asphyxia)
VVF (vesicovaginal fistula)Birth injuries
PPH (uterine atony)Meconium aspiration
Puerperal sepsisCerebral damage
Maternal deathNeonatal death

Management

  • STOP oxytocin immediately
  • 2 large-bore IV lines; IV fluids (NS/RL)
  • Catheterise bladder
  • IV antibiotics (ampicillin + gentamicin + metronidazole)
  • Crossmatch 2 units blood
  • If cervix not fully dilated: emergency C-section
  • If dead fetus + fully dilated: craniotomy (specialist)
  • If uterine rupture: emergency laparotomy
  • Prevention: Partograph use + timely referral
Mnemonic: Signs — "BOMB"
B=Bandl’s ring | O=Oedema of cervix | M=Moulding +3 | B=Blood in urine
Quick Revision
  • Obstructed labour = presenting part cannot descend despite adequate contractions due to mechanical barrier
  • Signs: Bandl’s ring, moulding +3, maternal exhaustion
  • Management: stop oxytocin, IV fluids, catheterise, emergency C-section.
Keywords: Obstructed labourBandl’s ringCPDUterine ruptureVVFPartograph

In Short: Prolonged labour = active phase >12h (primi) or >8h (multi). Cervix dilates <1 cm/hr. Causes: 3 P’s (Power, Passage, Passenger). Diagnosis: partograph. Management: hydration, oxytocin for power; C-section for passage.

Causes — 3 P’s

PCauses
PowerHypotonic contractions; incoordinate contractions; maternal exhaustion/dehydration
PassageContracted pelvis; pelvic tumour; cervical rigidity
PassengerMalposition (OP); malpresentation (brow); macrosomia; hydrocephalus

Management

  • General: IV fluids, empty bladder, emotional support
  • Power problem: ARM + oxytocin augmentation (5 IU in 500mL NS)
  • Passage problem: C-section (NEVER give oxytocin if obstruction suspected)
  • Passenger: Correct malposition; C-section if needed
  • Partograph monitoring; early referral when alert line crossed

In Short: Malpresentation = any presentation other than vertex. Breech (most common, 3–4%): frank (65%), complete (25%), footling (10%). Management: ECV at 37 wks; C-section for footling. Complications: head entrapment, cord prolapse.

Types of Breech

TypePositionFrequencyVaginal Delivery?
FrankHips flexed, knees extended65%Safest for vaginal delivery
CompleteHips and knees flexed25%Moderate risk
FootlingOne/both feet present first10%NEVER — always C-section

Etiology

  • Prematurity (most common cause)
  • Multiparity (lax uterus)
  • Uterine anomalies
  • Multiple pregnancy
  • Placenta praevia
  • Polyhydramnios/oligohydramnios

Management

  • Before 36 wks: watch (may convert spontaneously)
  • At 37 wks: ECV (External Cephalic Version); success ~50%
  • C-section if: footling, primigravida, EFW >3.8 kg, hyperextended head
  • Vaginal if: frank breech, EFW 2.5–3.5 kg, experienced team
  • Manoeuvres: Lovset (arms), Burns-Marshall/MSV (head), Piper forceps
  • Key rule: “Hands off the breech” — let buttocks deliver spontaneously

Complications

  • Head entrapment (most feared)
  • Cord prolapse (especially footling)
  • Birth asphyxia
  • Erb’s palsy
  • Fractures
Mnemonic: Complications — "CATCH"
C=Cord prolapse | A=Asphyxia | T=Trauma | C=Cervical tears | H=Head entrapment
Quick Revision

  • Breech (most common, 3–4%): frank (65%), complete (25%), footling (10%)
  • Management: ECV at 37 wks; C-section for footling
  • Complications: head entrapment, cord prolapse.

In Short: Occipitoposterior (OP) = fetal occiput facing mother’s sacrum (back-to-back). Causes severe backache labour. 65% rotate to OA spontaneously. If persistent: manual rotation, Kielland’s forceps, or C-section.

OutcomeFrequencyDescription
Long anterior rotation65%Occiput rotates 135° to OA → normal delivery
Short posterior rotation10%45° to direct OP → face-to-pubis delivery
Deep transverse arrest15%Stuck at transverse → instrumental/C-section
Persistent OP10%No rotation → operative delivery

Clinical Features

  • Flattened abdomen; fetal limbs felt anteriorly; FHR in flank
  • Severe backache during labour (hallmark)
  • Delayed engagement; prolonged 1st and 2nd stages
  • Early urge to push before full dilation

Management

  • Left lateral or all-fours position (encourages rotation)
  • Counter-pressure on sacrum for pain relief
  • If persistent: manual rotation, Kielland’s forceps, vacuum, or C-section

In Short: Obstetric emergencies: eclampsia, cord prolapse, PPH, shoulder dystocia, uterine rupture, AFE, uterine inversion. Footling breech is the most dangerous breech type (foot slips through undilated cervix → head trapped).

Obstetric Emergencies

EmergencyKey FeatureImmediate Action
EclampsiaConvulsionsMgSO4 + left lateral
Cord prolapseCord below presenting partPush head UP + knee-chest + C-section
PPHBleeding >500 mLRub uterus + oxytocin + IV fluids
Shoulder dystociaShoulders stuckMcRoberts + suprapubic pressure
Uterine ruptureSudden pain + shockEmergency laparotomy
AFECollapse + respiratory failure + DICCPR + intubation + ICU
Uterine inversionUterus inside outJohnson’s manoeuvre + IV fluids

Footling Breech

  • Foot slips through incompletely dilated cervix
  • Body delivers but aftercoming head trapped by cervix
  • Cord compressed between head and cervix → asphyxia within minutes
  • Management: always elective C-section for footling breech
  • If head trapped: Dührssen’s incisions (cervical cuts) by specialist

In Short: Obstetric emergencies = sudden life-threatening conditions. Key ones: eclampsia (MgSO4), cord prolapse (push head UP), PPH (rub uterus), shoulder dystocia (McRoberts), uterine rupture (laparotomy), AFE (CPR+ICU).

EmergencyFirst Nursing Action
EclampsiaLeft lateral + MgSO4 4g IV
Cord prolapsePush presenting part UP + knee-chest + emergency C-section

Shoulder dystociaMcRoberts + suprapubic pressure
Uterine ruptureIV fluids + crossmatch + emergency laparotomy
AFECPR + intubation + ICU
Uterine inversionJohnson’s repositioning + IV fluids

Nurse’s Role

  • Recognise emergency early; call for help
  • Initiate first-line management simultaneously
  • Maintain IV access, vitals, safe positioning
  • Keep emergency drugs available: oxytocin, MgSO4, calcium gluconate, adrenaline
  • Document all actions and timings

In Short: Obstetric emergencies demand instant action. Cord prolapse: push presenting part UP, knee-chest, fill bladder 500mL saline, emergency C-section. PPH: rub uterus. Eclampsia: MgSO4.

Emergency Management Table

EmergencyPresentationImmediate Management
EclampsiaConvulsions + hypertensionMgSO4 (Pritchard); left lateral; deliver in 12–24h
PPHBleeding >500mLFundal massage; oxytocin; IV fluids; bimanual compression
Cord prolapseCord felt/seen after ROMPush head UP; knee-chest; fill bladder 500mL; emergency C-section
Shoulder dystociaHead delivered, shoulders stuckHELPERR: McRoberts + suprapubic pressure + episiotomy

Cord Prolapse Detail (PUSH)

In Short: Cord prolapse = cord descends below presenting part after ROM. Obstetric emergency — fetal death in minutes. Management: push head UP, knee-chest, fill bladder 500mL, keep cord warm, emergency C-section.

Definitions

Cord Presentation: Cord felt through intact membranes ahead of presenting part.

Cord Prolapse: Cord descends past presenting part after ROM. OBSTETRIC EMERGENCY.

Occult Prolapse: Cord alongside presenting part — detected by variable decelerations on CTG.

Risk Factors

  • Malpresentation (breech, transverse)
  • Unengaged head at ROM
  • Polyhydramnios; preterm baby
  • ARM with high station
  • Long cord (>75cm)
  • After delivery of first twin

Emergency Management (PUSH)

  • CALL FOR HELP — activate emergency team
  • Push presenting part UP with hand in vagina (maintain continuously)
  • Knee-chest position or exaggerated Trendelenburg
  • Fill bladder with 500–700mL warm saline via Foley
  • Keep cord warm and moist (saline-soaked gauze)
  • Tocolytic (terbutaline 0.25mg SC) if available
  • Emergency C-section within minutes
  • If fully dilated + head low: forceps/vacuum
  • Do NOT push cord back (causes vasospasm)
Mnemonic: Management — "PUSH"
P=Push UP | U=Urgently position | S=Saline to bladder | H=Hurry to OT
Quick Revision

In Short: AFE = amniotic fluid enters maternal circulation → anaphylactoid reaction → cardiorespiratory collapse + DIC. Mortality 60–80%. Triad: respiratory distress + cardiovascular collapse + DIC. Treatment: CPR + intubation + blood products + ICU.

  • DIC: FFP, cryoprecipitate, platelets, packed RBCs
  • Transfer to ICU; mechanical ventilation
  • Emergency C-section if baby undelivered

In Short: C-section rate rising (WHO ideal: 10–15%). Factors: defensive medicine, repeat C-sections, CTG overuse, maternal request, ART pregnancies, reduced training in vaginal breech/forceps. Mother preparation: physical (NPO, IV, prep) + psychological (counselling, consent).

  • Increased CTG use → more interventions
  • Repeat C-sections (once C-section, likely again)
  • Reduced training in vaginal breech and forceps delivery
  • Older mothers; higher BMI; ART (precious baby)
  • Defensive medicine (fear of litigation)
  • Maternal request (tocophobia)
  • More inductions (failed inductions end in C-section)

Preparation of Mother

  • Physical: CBC, crossmatch, NPO, IV access, Foley, shave, TED stockings, antibiotics
  • Psychological: Explain procedure in simple language; address fears; informed consent; birth companion if possible; reassure about breastfeeding and bonding
  • Logistical: Blood arranged; neonatal team standby; OT readiness

In Short: Puerperal sepsis = infection of the genital tract after delivery. Caused by: poor asepsis, prolonged labour, PROM, retained products. Signs: fever ≥38°C, foul lochia, uterine tenderness. Management: IV antibiotics, remove source.

Definition

Puerperal Sepsis: Infection of the genital tract occurring within 42 days of delivery, manifested by fever ≥38°C on any 2 of the first 10 days postpartum (excluding first 24 hours).

Causes

  • Poor asepsis during delivery or examinations
  • Prolonged labour; prolonged rupture of membranes (>18h)
  • Retained products of conception
  • Operative delivery (C-section, forceps)
  • Multiple vaginal examinations
  • Anaemia; malnutrition; HIV

Clinical Features

  • Fever ≥38°C (may be persistent or spiking)
  • Offensive (foul-smelling) lochia
  • Uterine tenderness and subinvolution
  • Lower abdominal pain; tachycardia; malaise
  • May progress to peritonitis, pelvic abscess, septicaemia, septic shock

Management

  • IV antibiotics: Ampicillin + Gentamicin + Metronidazole (triple regimen)
  • IV fluids; antipyretics
  • Remove retained products (evacuation)
  • Wound care (if post-C-section wound infection)
  • Blood culture; high vaginal swab
  • Monitor vitals, urine output, lochia

Nursing Management

  • Monitor temperature q4h; vital signs; lochia (amount, colour, odour)
  • Maintain IV access; administer antibiotics as ordered
  • Perineal hygiene; frequent pad changes
  • Encourage oral fluids and nutrition
  • Isolate if necessary; hand hygiene
  • Emotional support; promote breastfeeding (usually safe to continue)
  • Prevention: Hand hygiene, aseptic technique, limit vaginal exams, timely delivery, prophylactic antibiotics for C-section
Quick Revision

  • Caused by: poor asepsis, prolonged labour, PROM, retained products
  • Signs: fever ≥38°C, foul lochia, uterine tenderness
  • Management: IV antibiotics, remove source.
Keywords: Puerperal sepsisPostpartum infectionTriple antibioticsRetained productsPrevention

In Short: PPH = blood loss ≥500mL after vaginal delivery or ≥1000mL after C-section. Causes: 4 T’s (Tone 70%, Trauma 20%, Tissue 10%, Thrombin <1%). Primary PPH within 24h; Secondary PPH 24h–6 weeks. Management: rub uterus + oxytocin + IV fluids.

Definition

PPH: Blood loss ≥500 mL after vaginal delivery or ≥1000 mL after C-section within 24 hours (primary PPH) or between 24 hours and 6 weeks (secondary PPH).

Causes — 4 T’s

CauseFrequencyExamples
Tone (Atony)70%Overdistended uterus (twins, polyhydramnios, macrosomia), prolonged labour, oxytocin use, grand multipara
Trauma20%Cervical/vaginal/perineal tears, uterine rupture, episiotomy extension
Tissue10%Retained placenta, retained membranes/clots, placenta accreta
Thrombin<1%DIC, coagulopathy, anticoagulant use

Management

  • First action: Rub up the uterus (fundal massage)
  • Empty bladder (catheterise)
  • Oxytocin 10 IU IM + 20 IU in 500mL NS drip
  • Methergin 0.2mg IM (if not hypertensive)
  • Misoprostol 800mcg sublingual/rectal
  • IV fluids: NS/RL 2L fast; crossmatch; transfuse if needed
  • Bimanual compression: If uterus still atonic
  • Aortic compression: Press fist above umbilicus (buys time)
  • If retained placenta: manual removal under anaesthesia
  • Surgical options: B-Lynch suture, uterine artery ligation, hysterectomy (last resort)
  • AMTSL: Best prevention (oxytocin after delivery of anterior shoulder + CCT + uterine massage)

Nursing Management

  • Monitor vitals every 15 min; urine output; consciousness
  • Maintain 2 large-bore IV lines
  • Keep patient warm (prevent hypothermia)
  • Blood samples: CBC, crossmatch, coagulation
  • Documentation: blood loss estimation, interventions, timing
  • Emotional support to patient and family
Mnemonic: Causes — "4 T’s"
T=Tone (70%, most common) | T=Trauma (20%) | T=Tissue (10%) | T=Thrombin (<1%)

In Short: PPH signs: excessive vaginal bleeding, rising pulse, falling BP, pallor, cold clammy skin, air hunger. Management: rub uterus + oxytocin + IV fluids + crossmatch. Nursing: monitor vitals q15min, maintain 2 IV lines.

Signs & Symptoms

  • Excessive vaginal bleeding (may be concealed in uterus)
  • Rising pulse rate (tachycardia, early sign)
  • Falling BP (hypotension, late sign)
  • Pallor; cold, clammy skin; diaphoresis
  • Air hunger; restlessness; altered consciousness
  • Soft, boggy uterus (if atonic)
  • Estimated blood loss: soaked pad = ~100mL; clots = significant

Step-wise Management

  • SHOUT for help; do not leave the patient
  • Rub up uterus; empty bladder
  • Oxytocin 10 IU IM + drip; methergin; misoprostol
  • 2 large-bore IV lines; NS/RL 2L fast
  • Crossmatch 4 units; transfuse if Hb <7 or clinically shocked
  • Bimanual compression if atonic
  • Surgical options if medical management fails

Nursing Management

  • Monitor vitals every 15 minutes
  • Maintain 2 IV accesses; strict I/O charting
  • Estimate blood loss (weigh pads, count soaked pads)
  • Keep patient warm; oxygen if SpO2 <95%
  • Send blood: CBC, crossmatch, coagulation profile
  • Emotional support; explain situation to family

In Short: Case-based: Mrs. S, 2500g baby at 10:30 PM, complains of bleeding. Likely primary PPH. Assess: fundal height, uterine tone, vital signs, perineal tears. Management: rub uterus, oxytocin, IV fluids, identify cause (4 T’s).

Case Assessment

  • History: Delivered 2500g male baby; complains of bleeding post-delivery
  • Immediate assessment: Check uterine tone (is it boggy?); vital signs; estimated blood loss; perineal inspection for tears
  • Most likely cause: Uterine atony (most common cause of primary PPH)

Management

  • Rub up the uterus (fundal massage)
  • Empty bladder (catheterise)
  • Oxytocin 10 IU IM + 20 IU in NS drip
  • If not hypertensive: methergin 0.2mg IM
  • IV fluids: 2L NS/RL fast
  • If perineal tear: identify degree and repair
  • If retained products: manual removal under anaesthesia
  • Crossmatch blood; transfuse if needed

Nursing Care Plan

  • Monitor vitals every 15 min until stable
  • Strict I/O chart; aim for urine output ≥30 mL/hr
  • Continue fundal massage for 2 hours post-delivery
  • Check fundal height every 15 min (should be at or below umbilicus)
  • Emotional support; reassurance
  • Encourage early breastfeeding (stimulates oxytocin release)

In Short: Breast engorgement occurs day 3–4 postpartum due to milk stasis. Signs: hard, painful, warm, tense breasts. Management: frequent breastfeeding (best treatment), warm compress before feed, cold compress after, analgesics, hand expression.

In Short: Premature baby = born before 37 weeks or weight <2500g. Causes: PIH, infections, multiple pregnancy, APH, cervical incompetence. Problems: RDS, hypothermia, hypoglycaemia, jaundice, NEC, IVH. Care: KMC, incubator, gavage feeds.

In Short: Preterm baby care: thermoregulation (KMC, incubator), feeding (breast milk, gavage if needed), infection prevention (hand hygiene, minimal handling), respiratory support, monitoring (temp, weight, glucose, bilirubin).

In Short: KMC (Kangaroo Mother Care) = skin-to-skin contact between mother and baby. Baby placed between breasts in frog position, covered with wrapper. Benefits: thermoregulation, breastfeeding, bonding, reduces infection and mortality. Criteria: stable baby, mother willing.

KMC Components

  • Position: Baby in upright position between mother’s breasts, skin-to-skin, in frog position
  • Clothing: Baby wears only cap, socks, diaper; mother’s clothes/wrapper over both
  • Duration: 24/7 if possible; minimum 1 hour at a time
  • Feeding: Exclusive breastfeeding; baby can feed in KMC position

Benefits

  • Thermoregulation (maintains temperature)
  • Promotes breastfeeding and weight gain
  • Reduces infections and hospital stay
  • Improves bonding and maternal confidence
  • Stabilises heart rate and breathing
  • Reduces neonatal mortality by up to 40%

Criteria

  • Stable baby (no critical illness, no ventilator)
  • Baby weighing <2500g (especially <1800g)
  • Mother willing and trained; family support
  • Can be done by father or any family member

In Short: Postmaturity = pregnancy beyond 42 weeks (294 days). Signs: dry peeling skin, absent vernix, meconium staining, long nails, alert look. Risks: meconium aspiration, cord compression, macrosomia, stillbirth. Manage: induce at 41 weeks.

In Short: Asphyxia neonatorum = failure to initiate and sustain breathing at birth. Causes: maternal (APH, PIH), fetal (prematurity, cord prolapse), labour (prolonged, obstructed). Management: NRP (Neonatal Resuscitation Protocol) — stimulate, suction, bag-mask, intubate.

In Short: Prevention of HIV in newborn: PPTCT (Prevention of Parent-to-Child Transmission). ART to mother during pregnancy + during labour + to newborn (nevirapine syrup). Avoid breastfeeding if replacement feeding feasible; else exclusive BF for 6 months.

  • Intrapartum: Continue ART; avoid prolonged ROM and invasive procedures
  • Postnatal (baby): Nevirapine syrup from birth for 6–12 weeks
  • Feeding: If safe replacement feeding available: formula feed; If not available: exclusive breastfeeding for 6 months (mixed feeding increases risk)
  • Elective C-section: If viral load >1000 copies/mL (reduces transmission)
  • Testing: DNA PCR test for baby at 6 weeks and 6 months

In Short: Counselling HIV-positive mother: discuss PPTCT, ART compliance, delivery plan, infant feeding options (AFASS criteria), follow-up testing, contraception, partner testing, emotional support, confidentiality.

In Short: Fertilisation = fusion of sperm and ovum in ampulla of fallopian tube. Implantation = embedding of blastocyst in endometrium on day 6–7. Spermatogenesis produces sperm in testes (74 days). Oogenesis produces ova in ovaries (from fetal life).

Definitions

Fertilisation: Union of sperm and ovum in the ampulla of the fallopian tube, forming a zygote.

Implantation: Embedding of the blastocyst into the endometrium on day 6–7 after fertilisation.

Spermatogenesis vs Oogenesis

FeatureSpermatogenesisOogenesis
SiteSeminiferous tubules of testesOvary
Duration74 days per cycleStarts in fetal life; completes at ovulation
Output200–300 million/day1 ovum per cycle
Cell typeSpermatogonium → spermatocyte → spermatid → spermOogonium → primary oocyte → secondary oocyte → ovum
HormonesFSH + testosteroneFSH + LH + oestrogen
BeginsPubertyFetal life (arrested until puberty)
EndsLifelong (decreases with age)Menopause

Steps of Fertilisation

  • Sperm capacitation in female genital tract (6–7 hours)
  • Sperm penetrates corona radiata and zona pellucida
  • Acrosome reaction releases enzymes to enter ovum
  • Cortical reaction prevents polyspermy (zona block)
  • Fusion of male and female pronuclei → zygote (46 chromosomes)
  • Zygote undergoes mitotic division → morula → blastocyst → implants in endometrium
Quick Revision
  • Fertilisation = fusion of sperm and ovum in ampulla of fallopian tube
  • Implantation = embedding of blastocyst in endometrium on day 6–7
  • Spermatogenesis produces sperm in testes (74 days)
  • Oogenesis produces ova in ovaries (from fetal life).

In Short: Case: Mrs. S, 27 years, married 6 years, regular unprotected intercourse, no conception = primary infertility. Investigation: both partners. Female: history, HSG, hormonal assays. Male: semen analysis. Treatment depends on cause.

Definition

Primary Infertility: Failure to conceive after 1 year of regular, unprotected intercourse without any previous conception.
Secondary Infertility: Failure to conceive after a previous pregnancy (whether live birth or not).

Investigations

PartnerInvestigations
FemaleHistory + examination; Day 2 FSH/LH/E2; Day 21 progesterone (ovulation confirmation); USG (follicular tracking); HSG (tubal patency); thyroid function; prolactin
MaleSemen analysis (count, motility, morphology, volume); Normal: ≥15 million/mL, ≥40% motility; hormonal assay if abnormal

Causes & Treatment

  • Ovulatory dysfunction (25%): Clomiphene citrate; gonadotropins; lifestyle modification
  • Tubal factor (25%): Tubal surgery; IVF if blocked
  • Male factor (30%): Treat infection; IUI; ICSI if severe
  • Unexplained (15%): IUI; IVF
  • Endometriosis: Laparoscopic surgery; GnRH agonists; IVF

Nursing Role

  • Detailed history of both partners
  • Emotional support (infertility causes significant distress)
  • Education about fertile period and timing of intercourse
  • Support through treatment procedures; manage expectations
  • Counselling about ART options and success rates
Quick Revision

  • Female: history, HSG, hormonal assays

In Short: Surrogacy Bill (India, 2021): Only altruistic surrogacy allowed (no commercial). Surrogate must be close relative, married, have own child. Intended parents must be Indian married couple (25–50 wife, 26–55 husband). Bans single parents, live-in couples, foreigners.

Key Provisions

  • Only altruistic surrogacy allowed (no payment beyond medical expenses)
  • Commercial surrogacy is banned
  • Surrogate must be: close relative, married, have her own child, age 25–35
  • Intended parents: Indian married couple married for 5 years; wife 25–50, husband 26–55
  • Must have medical indication for surrogacy (certificate from district medical board)
  • Single parents, live-in couples, and foreigners cannot opt for surrogacy
  • National and State Surrogacy Boards constituted for regulation

237:
In Short: Uterine prolapse = descent of uterus into or beyond the vaginal canal. Degrees: 1st (descent within vagina), 2nd (cervix at introitus), 3rd (complete prolapse outside). Causes: multiparity, weak pelvic floor. Treatment: pessary or surgery (hysterectomy, repair).

Definition

Uterine Prolapse: Descent of the uterus from its normal anatomical position downward into the vaginal canal, due to weakness of pelvic floor muscles and ligaments.

Degrees

DegreeDescription
1st degreeCervix descends into upper vagina (above introitus)
2nd degreeCervix reaches the introitus (vaginal opening)
3rd degree (Procidentia)Entire uterus protrudes outside the vagina

Causes

  • Multiparity (most common cause)
  • Prolonged labour; precipitate labour
  • Weak pelvic floor (menopause, oestrogen deficiency)
  • Chronic cough; constipation; heavy lifting (increase abdominal pressure)
  • Congenital weakness of pelvic ligaments

Management

  • Conservative: Pelvic floor exercises (Kegel); Ring pessary (elderly/unfit for surgery)
  • Pre-operative care: Vaginal hygiene; treat ulceration; estrogen cream; treat UTI; optimise health
  • Surgery: Anterior colporrhaphy (for cystocele); posterior colpoperineorrhaphy (for rectocele); Manchester repair (amputation of cervix + repair); Vaginal hysterectomy (definitive treatment)
  • Post-operative care: Monitor vitals; catheter care; wound care; avoid straining; pelvic floor exercises; avoid heavy lifting for 3 months
Quick Revision

  • Degrees: 1st (descent within vagina), 2nd (cervix at introitus), 3rd (complete prolapse outside)
  • Causes: multiparity, weak pelvic floor
  • Treatment: pessary or surgery (hysterectomy, repair).
